topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon sun beat down, highlighting the dust motes dancing in the air as I watched Mr. Henderson meticulously measure the posts for his new wooden barrier. Every swing of his hammer sounded unnervingly loud in the otherwise quiet street. I stood by my window, my knuckles white where I gripped the sill, the sound of his work echoing in my ears like a judgment. I kept glancing toward his yard, my stomach twisting with a heavy, unpleasant feeling. I wanted to say something, to interrupt the progress, but the words caught in my throat, solid and unmoving. He paused, wiping sweat from his brow, and offered a brief, bright smile. That small gesture only made the knot in my chest tighten further.

[story 2]

I spent the entire morning rearranging my garden tools, a frantic, pointless exercise in order. Each time I looked toward the shared property line, a flush crept up my neck, warm and undeniable. The thought of that new fence going up—imposing, permanent—made my breath catch. I kept fiddling with the latch on my back door, my fingers trembling slightly. When Mr. Henderson knocked, his enthusiasm was infectious, all booming laughter and plans for the perfect boundary. I managed a strained, "Oh, that sounds wonderful," the words tasting like ash in my mouth. I focused intently on smoothing a patch of wilting petunias, desperate to disappear into the green foliage.

[story 3]

The smell of fresh-cut lumber hung heavy in the air, a sharp, woody scent that seemed to permeate the very atmosphere of our neighborhood. I found myself pacing the length of my living room floor, my movements jerky and restless. Every time I closed my eyes, I could picture the planks going up, a solid wall dividing our small patch of shared space. I kept running a hand over my face, feeling the heat rising beneath my skin. When Mr. Henderson stopped by to ask if I needed anything, his cheerful tone seemed too loud, too insistent. I just nodded, a jerky motion, unable to meet his gaze, focusing instead on the pattern of the worn rug beneath my feet.
